title: Being a Person Who Uses Technology Well

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Review of all Stretching Branches mentor guides from 4.2.1-A through 4.2.6-A

narration_prompts.prompt: Can the child explain what happens when they use a computer?
narration_prompts.detail: Not technical perfection — but basic understanding that the internet is physical, files are stored somewhere real, and what appears on screen was put there by someone.

narration_prompts.prompt: Does the child keep information private?
narration_prompts.detail: Are passwords strong and not shared? Does the child pause before clicking unexpected links? Do they question app permissions?

narration_prompts.prompt: Does the child question what they see online?
narration_prompts.detail: When encountering content, do they ask who decided to show it and whether it is true? Can they identify examples of how content can be misleading?

narration_prompts.prompt: Has the child created something with a digital tool?
narration_prompts.detail: Is there evidence of a builder orientation — completed projects, programs, creative work?

narration_prompts.prompt: Does the child understand that AI is a tool that can be wrong?
narration_prompts.detail: Can they articulate the difference between pattern recognition and understanding?

narration_prompts.prompt: Does the child use screens with intention?
narration_prompts.detail: Can they define a purpose before using a device? Can they stop without a struggle? Do they demonstrate boredom tolerance?

cross_connections.description: When the student advances to Weathering Storms, these integrated habits become the foundation for a formal audit and systematic sovereignty assessment.

content.why_this_matters:
You have learned a lot about computers and the digital world. You know that computers are machines that follow instructions. You know how to keep your information safe with strong passwords. You know that not everything you see online is true. You know that AI finds patterns instead of thinking. And you know that screens are tools to use on purpose, not default activities to fill time.

This packet brings everything together. It is not about learning new things — it is about showing that all the things you have learned work together in your daily life. A person who uses technology well does not just know individual facts. They have habits that work together: they understand how computers work, they keep themselves safe, they question what they see, they use AI carefully, and they use screens with purpose.

That is what digital sovereignty means at your age: being a person who uses technology thoughtfully and deliberately, not a person who is used by technology without thinking.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Integrated digital habits** — all six prior sub-domains functioning together, not as separate skills
2. **The ability to explain how technology works** to someone else in simple terms
3. **Demonstrated information safety** as a consistent practice, not an occasional behavior
4. **Questioning habits** that are applied naturally when encountering digital content
5. **A creation orientation** — using computers to make things as part of regular life
6. **Intentional screen use** that is observed over time, not tested in a single moment
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Putting It All Together

You have learned six important things about the digital world:

1. **Computers are machines.** They follow instructions written by people. They do not think. What you see on screen is a designed presentation. (4.2.1-A)

2. **Safety is a habit.** Strong passwords, private information discipline, pause before clicking, and questioning permissions protect you online. (4.2.2-A)

3. **Not everything you see is true.** Someone chose to show it to you. Popular does not mean true. Content can be edited or taken out of context. (4.2.3-A)

4. **You can make things.** Computers are creation tools — for writing, drawing, programming, and building. The builder mindset treats technology as raw material. (4.2.4-A)

5. **AI is a tool, not a thinker.** It finds patterns. It can be wrong. People build it and their choices matter. Never trust it automatically. (4.2.5-A)

6. **Screens are for purposes, not defaults.** The pull is designed. Boredom is useful. You can notice the pull and choose how to respond. (4.2.6-A)

Each of these is valuable on its own. Together, they form something more powerful: a way of being in the digital world that is informed, careful, creative, and deliberate. That is digital sovereignty.

### Part 2: Showing What You Know

This is not a test with right and wrong answers. It is a chance to demonstrate that you live these ideas — that they have become part of how you actually use technology, not just things you can say when asked.

**Demonstration activities:**

**Explain it to someone.** Pick one concept from the list above and explain it to a family member or friend who might not know it. Use a digital tool to make your explanation — a presentation, a short video, or a drawing. Teaching something to someone else is the best way to show you understand it.

**Show your safety habits.** Walk through your passwords — are they strong? Are they different for different accounts? Show how you would handle a suspicious message. Show your device permissions.

**Show your creation work.** What have you made with a computer? Pull up your files. Show your Scratch programs, your stories, your drawings, your presentations. A person who uses technology well has created things.

**Show your questioning habit.** Look at something together on a screen — a video, a post, a search result. Talk through what you see: who decided to show you this? Is it true? How could you check? Do the view counts matter?

**Show your screen discipline.** Can you define your purpose before picking up a screen? Can you stop when the purpose is fulfilled? Can you sit with boredom without reaching for a device? These are observed over time, not in a single moment.

### Part 3: What Comes Next

As you grow older, everything you have learned here will deepen. You will learn exactly how the internet routes information. You will learn how to identify phishing with precision. You will understand how algorithms create different realities for different people. You will program in real languages and build real projects. You will use AI tools effectively while checking everything they produce. You will understand the neuroscience of attention and build a deep work practice.

But all of that builds on what you have learned now. The child who understands that a computer is a machine, not magic, is ready for everything that comes next. The habits you have built — strong passwords, questioning what you see, creating instead of only consuming, using screens with purpose — these are the foundation. They do not change as you grow older. They only deepen.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Integrated Behavior
**Developing well:** The child's digital behavior shows all six prior areas functioning together. They use technology with purpose, maintain safety habits, question content, create things, understand AI's limitations, and manage screen time — not as separate exercises but as a natural, integrated way of being.

**Developing slowly:** Individual skills exist but do not function together. The child maintains passwords but does not question content. Or they question content but cannot manage screen time.

**Diagnostic approach:** Observe the child across a normal week of digital use. Note which habits are consistent, which are sporadic, and which are absent.

### 2. Teaching Capability
**Developing well:** The child can explain a concept from 4.2 to someone else in clear, simple terms. The show-and-tell demonstrates real understanding, not memorized phrases.

**Developing slowly:** The child repeats phrases from the packets without genuine understanding. They cannot answer follow-up questions or explain in different words.

### 3. Creation Orientation
**Developing well:** The child has a portfolio of completed digital creations — programs, stories, drawings, presentations. Creation is part of how they use computers.

**Developing slowly:** The child's computer use is primarily consumption — watching, scrolling, playing games made by others — with little creation.
